items.q: How is Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) calculated?
items.correct: Operating profit divided by capital employed (total equity + non-current liabilities), expressed as a percentage. It measures how efficiently the business uses its long-term financing to generate profit.
items.misconception: ROCE is just net profit divided by revenue.
items.random_wrong: ROCE is the wages divided by hours worked.
items.why: ROCE compares profit to capital used, not revenue. It distinguishes capital-efficient businesses from revenue-heavy but capital-intensive ones. Students often confuse it with profit margin.

items.q: What is the difference between gross profit and net profit?
items.correct: Gross profit = revenue minus cost of goods sold (direct costs only); net profit = revenue minus ALL costs including overheads, interest and tax. Net profit is the bottom line.
items.misconception: Gross profit and net profit are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Gross profit is profit before VAT and net profit is profit after VAT.
items.why: The gap between gross and net reveals overhead burden. A high gross but low net margin means heavy fixed costs or financing. Students conflate the two and misread profitability.

items.correct: The level of output where total revenue equals total costs — neither profit nor loss. It is NOT the level where profit is maximised; profit grows beyond break-even.
items.misconception: Break-even is where the business makes the most profit.
items.random_wrong: Break-even is where the business owes no debt.
items.why: Break-even is a no-profit-no-loss point, useful for risk analysis. Students conflate it with profit maximisation. Going above break-even is where profit starts; below break-even means a loss.
Correct answer: Operating profit / capital employed x 100. “Net profit / sales x 100” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If gross margin is 60 percent and net margin is 5 percent, overheads and interest are:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Large. “Negligible” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Operating profit 60, capital employed 300. ROCE percent?
items.why: The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
